speaker: interviewee
text: --out twice--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --without reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All right. You were discharged from the army, and you find your mother and sister. We know that you-- you remained in Poland at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remained in Poland at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You eventually married.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in August of 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ingeborge [? Erika, ?] nee-- maiden name Barth, B-E-- B-A-R-T-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Poland in various places. First in the place where I met my mother. And then I moved in the same county in the town. And then subsequently, I moved back to Upper Silesia [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because we had problems with my son. And I was working, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we needed to go somewhere where somebody could take care of, of my, my son. And so Upper Silesia was-- was a better place.

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to introduce your son, whose name is-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of my son is George Andre. He was born in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where at?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that same village where my mother was in the concentration camp and where I married my wife, which was at that time called Peterswaldau, [NON-ENGLISH] Pieszyce when he was born, P-E-- P-I-E-S-Z-Y-C-E. And he lives now in Rocky River, Ohio. He has a master's degree in political science. His wife has a PhD in clinical psychology. They have two wonderful children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Andre [? Markus, ?] after me and my great-grandfather, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Geneveve [? Camille. ?] Andre is 7, and Geneveve is-- we call her Veva-- is 5. And he is in first grade, and she is in kindergarten. And my son works for a company here, in parking enclosures, as a sales manager. And my daughter-in-law has-- is in private practice a little bit. And she works  for the Veterans Administration.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your wife and your son tried to get out of Poland several times?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got-- that first time I tried to get-- I need-- for a visit to my mother's cousin in Sweden. I wanted to meet my-- when-- my, my mother in Sweden when things eased up in Poland after 1956. Because in 1956, there was a big turnover in Poland, and things eased up a little bit. My mother wouldn't come to Poland. We had a cousin in Sweden. The Poles denied me the passport. The second time, we applied of a passport to go to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because my mother had applied for a visa first to come to America, but at the time, the American immigration law was such that I would have to come alone as the son of an already nationalized American citizen. My wife and my son would have to stay back in Poland until I got here. And I was afraid to leave them there. And the only way out for all of us was to go to Israel. I wasn't afraid to live in Israel. I know she could get out from there anytime. But while we were in Israel-- we got out of Poland finally in 1959. After I wrote to everybody-- after they had denied my passport a second time-- I wrote to everybody, including the president of the country. I did not give up. I was-- when I am determined to do something, I get it done. And so we got out. And we got to Israel. And we stayed 13 months in Israel, where we both worked. My wife was a housekeeper, and myself in a toy factory assembling tricycles. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there I started earnestly to learn English, to study English. I came to America in 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: When in 1960?

speaker: interviewee
text: April 22nd, 1960 aboard the good ship probably owned by Onassis. I think that was one of the Greek line-- he owned that, that whole thing, which we didn't know at the time. It was meaningless to us. The voyage took about two weeks. We had been staying for three days in, in, in Athens. Where the day before, the first time we stepped on the American continent was the day before in Halifax, Nova Scotia. It was bitter cold. We arrived in New York April 22nd-- 84 degrees-- in our winter clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] On April 22nd, 84 degrees isn't bad, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was hot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What has been your occupation since arriving in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mainly that-- what I did in Europe. I worked in-- since I came out of the army, in accountant-related jobs. Payroll, cost accounting, sometimes both, sometimes only one of them. When I came to Cleveland, the first three months I worked in a shop assembling lamps, and so-- for a company called Funeral Supply Company. Then I worked for eight years for a wholesaler of gasoline and did everything-- accounts payable, accounts receivable, tax reports on excise taxes, and, and so on. And then, for the last 18 years, I was in charge of cost accounting and payroll at a manufacturer of molded rubber products for the automobile aftermarket. I had to leave there in the end of 1986 when the company went belly-up. I still worked for them two years part-time when they had the reduced staff of ten people. And since then, I have been working temporarily sometimes for a week or two in, in assorted accounting jobs, just did three months for BP America in, in, in department of excise taxes. And I started to write. I had gotten a third prize in a story-writing contest in Germany with an institution. I have published about, oh, a little bit more than a dozen articles in a German paper. And I have published two parts of my memoirs on my childhood and boyhood in Katowice. The, the one is now being printed in Poland and-- with a-- in-- bilingual in German and Polish simultaneously, with a Polish translation which I made after I got one which was unsatisfactory to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would it be fair to say your grandkids are the light of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandkids, I tried to tell them some stuff. We have a problem. My, my daughter-in-law is American born. And as such, like many Americans, has an-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] does not-- is not very fond of foreign languages. We wanted to, to teach our grandchildren when they were little. And she was afraid that she might not understand them when we talked German to them. I don't know. And when they-- if-- because if we started off-- my, my grandson has a very good ear. And he can-- knows a few phrases. And he knows a few Polish phrases. And knew some Polish songs a, a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm sorry that they don't want to learn. And I'm not getting much support from-- either one of them. So what can I do? Shrug my shoulders.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm preparing the albums with photos which I have of my family with inscriptions a little so that they know who some of the people are, going back to my great-grandfather, my grandmother, grandparents, paternal grandparents, from my youth, from my mother's-- from my wife's youth, whatever I can. And where I find it proper, I put in some of the indications who the people are. I also am collecting what I can of my father's writings. And I have two larger articles in that book which he edited. So that if they want to know, then there is going to be a trace. If they don't want to know, I am going to send the stuff probably to the Leo Baeck Institute in New York. I spoke to a man over there a week ago. I still am going to try to find my father's doctor dissertation. I know where it was done and when it was done. But it was never, as is customary in Germany, printed. Because in 1919, there was no paper to print it on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what the Holocaust has meant to you in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, it meant that I lost a lot of my family. And I lost almost all my friends. This is very-- hits closest. I mean, it hits you if you know that a million people are being killed, or two million, or three million. By, by-- at a certain point, it doesn't make any difference any more if it's one million, or two million, or three million. I mean, a million people-- this is-- this is a lot-- this is a lot of people. But if it hits close to home, if it hits-- if I-- and I think you're going to show the list of the Gestapo from the city of Beuthen, where there's a date when my grandmother was taken away and sent out. And two of her-- two or three of her brothers are in that list, and assorted uncles, and-- and friends, and parents of friends. This is where it hits home. And if-- I came back in 1945 to the city where I was born as a solider. We drove through on a truck. We had a garrison 20 million-- 20 miles out of there. And you come-- and the next day, I was in the city going for the Jewish committee. And you come to the city where you were born, and there is not-- the city is very little destroyed, a few houses here and there. By and large it is whole. Because there's industries there they don't wish-- want to destroy. But there is not a single soul in that city whom you know. You are a stranger. This is something for-- Americans can't comprehend, that an American soldier goes to war, then he comes back. And there is nobody there in South Euclid, in Mayfield Heights, in-- wherever it is, in East Cleveland. All your friends-- you'll find some of them, oh, this one is in Israel. And one is maybe in Australia. And one is somewhere else. Some of the distances-- you're never go to see 'em again. You'll find, little by little, people. My mother gave-- wrote to the radio station in Warsaw her name. At the Red Cross was published names. And my aunt is standing in Haifa on a street corner and having her shoes polished, what she seldom did, and hears that my mother is alive, that they are now in Szeged. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I am having a good memory. And I remember that my aunt lived in Haifa, Ahuza, on Tel Mane 2. It's a short address. 'Tel Mane' means Mane Hill. And as a mere soldier-- and I remember-- remembered that. And as soon as I got into Poland, the mail is going. I write a letter to [NON-ENGLISH], Haifa, Ahuza, Tel Mane 2. They know that I am alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: My-- Is there--

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the girl I fell in love with in, in Tarnopol, who came three times to me to the train, I never-- I have rumors. People have seen there where she died, when she died, but nothing for sure. Your friends, every-- I mean, I was a member of the Zionist organization in Katowice from about 1934 to 1939. There is one guys in California who was the head of the organization in the town for a while. I have tried with him to take-- make contact. I couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me-- is there anything you'd like to say to future generations about this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] Never again. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] Sure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I'm grateful to Steven Spielberg [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] for making that possible. I have been seldom so moved as I was after watching [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not because of Schindler, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because of the people he showed-- Plaszow, the camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Schindler was an aberration, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] coincidence. I don't know if he was good or if he was greedy. It doesn't matter. I had a, at one time, a discussion with a man who said, I should be grateful to the Soviets because they saved my life. And I said, no. And I am not such a Jewish scholar that I knew that at the time, but I know it now. Because there is a Jewish law which says, the intent is important of a deed. The Russian intent was not to save my life. If the Russian intent had been to save my life, it would have been what is called in Hebrew, a mitzvah, a good deed. What saved me was not a good deed. It turned out to be against their intentions. Because I told you they told us, you're going to die here in Siberia. They didn't want to save us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Schindler remains, in a way, an enigma. Did he do it because he wanted to save? If you-- that was the reason, then he did a good deed. If otherwise, it was a coincidence.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even so-- and as a, a friend of my father's used to say, the result is-- of an action is important, which is a little bit contradictory with the Jewish interpretation. So it's all right. He saved people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But for Spielberg, first of all, to make the movie, and how he made it, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and for Mr. Williams to write the music, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and for Itzhak Perlman to play it, that helped. And that Steven Spielberg now take the money to make this project, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] this is a good deed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sitting here to do this project with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] I am glad I could able to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm glad you [? could. ?] Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell me the name?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my, my maternal great-grandfather, Adolf Becker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in Beuthen. Oh, that's probably in the late '20s-- late 1920s, beginning of 1930s. He died in 1937 at the age of 92.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was taken in Beuthen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Beuthen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is it that you have this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it came to my mother from one of her cousins who emigrated from Beuthen to Palestine in 1933. At that time, she could take-- they had a lot of money. They went as capitalists, and they could take everything with them. And so, since they had a lot of stuff, she gave some of it to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And describe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this a picture of?

words.text: who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my maternal grandparents, Eugene and Flora Wachsner. That is a wedding picture taken in Beuthen, Oberschlesien within Upper Silesia in 1899.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have this picture like you had the other one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- probably also came from, from my aunt in Israel. I am not 100% sure because many of those pictures I had from my mother when my mother passed away here in Cleveland. And some of them, of course, I don't know how she was able to get them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my paternal grandparents, Bernhard Guttmann and Olga Guttmann, nee [? Sachs. ?] That picture was taken already in Berlin when they were oh, probably in her-- short-- probably not too long before they were deported to Theresienstadt, probably around 1940. Again, it came from my mother's collection somewhere, possibly from my father's sister. That is possibly from my father's sister, who lived in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And who is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Dr. Fritz Guttmann, with my mother, Gertrude Guttmann, nee Wachsner. That is taken probably shortly after they got married around-- they got married in 1920. My father is very skinny over there. And the years when I remember him, he was rather heavy. [LAUGHS] So that is probably then. That came, in all probability, from my aunt from Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. OK. And who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my sister Vera [? Pauline. ?] She is about six months old there. That must have been in 1931. She was born in 1931. She was born in Beuthen. No-- but on-- that is still in our-- no, that was done at a professional photographer studio.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, this is a list-- a-- one page of a list from the Beuthen Oberschlesien Gestapo, which-- from the deportation of the last 940, about-- I think that's the last number there-- 950 Jews from that city, which took place between May 14, 1942 and beginning of June. On that page, on the line what's highlighted is my grandmother, my maternal grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how is it that you have this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have that list through a girl from-- who was from Beuthen who is in a kibbutz in Israel, who made a visit to Beuthen in the late '70s, beginning '80s, or beginning-- late '80s. How she obtained it in Beuthen, I don't know. But I was sent a copy of it from Israel from the kibbutz [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Get as close as you can, Matt.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, wait until Matt's ready--

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I'll lose it. OK. So I'm staying--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Max Guttmann, Sergeant Major in Polish Army, in the place of Duszniki in mountains near, near, near Lodz in the spring of 1946, either fall of '45 or spring of '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the synagogue in Beuthen, Oberschlesien, and where I was singing as a boy in the choir. And adjacent-- in one of the two houses adjacent to it, that was the place. It was called Schiesshausplatz. In one of those buildings, my mother was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture is taken from the book cover that was just shown preceding this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And the book was published in 1992 under the auspices of a foundation called Haus Oberschlesien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Max, thank you. What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. That's OK, not a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just, you know, poof--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. This is a--

speaker: interviewee
text: That is why-- of-- a kind of a substitute for a birth document. They, they-- people used to get books for their families where they could record the parent's weddings and birth of their children. This is what is called in German, [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: And there is in the front-- on the first page is a marriage certificate of my parents, and the second page, the birth certificates of my sister and myself. You want both of them? Or you're only going to page-- Just the one that's flat--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that's flat. Can you-- how do you have this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother's-- carried, schlepped it through the camps. How she was hiding it, I don't know. She must have had a way of hiding it, but she schlepped it through the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Thank you. Max, who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my grandchildren, Andre and Veva.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken probably-- how old are they? They're probably--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was about 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years, maybe, ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: 5 and 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in Cleveland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Rocky River.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of, I think, George, and my-- his wife, and my wife, and myself, and my little granddaughter. And Andre, the grandson, took the picture. That is in their new home, when-- which they moved in December of 1993 in Rocky River.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter-in-law's name is--

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter-in-law's name is Debra.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Ingeborge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your granddaughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My granddaughter's name is-- Veva, we call her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Geneveve.
